turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: We will hear argument first this morning in Case 24-872, Hamm versus Smith. Mr. Overing.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Nothing in the Eighth Amendment bars the sentence Joseph Smith received for murdering Durk Van Dam nearly 30 years ago. Atkins created an exception for offenders known to be intellectually disabled, but Smith is not. He didn't come close to proving an IQ of 70 or below with scores of 75, 74, 72, 78, and 74. But the lower courts changed the rules. First, misreading Hall, they took the lowest score to represent a possibility that Smith's IQ is 69. Then, to the extent they considered other scores, they took each only in isolation and moved the line to 75. These maneuvers expanded Atkins, and this Court should reverse that expansion, first, because it was not authorized by the Eighth Amendment, second, because it would eliminate state discretion, and, third, because it ignores that the combined effect of multiple IQ scores reduces error and yields a more accurate estimate. The answer to whether courts may consider the cumulative effect of multiple IQ scores is yes. The answer to how they handle multiple scores is not found in the Eighth Amendment and, thus, left to state discretion. Still, this Court can offer some meaningful ground rules for how to handle multiple scores. First, states are allowed to make the first prong turn on the very best evidence of intellectual functioning, IQ. Second, states are allowed to define the requisite intellectual functioning as an IQ of 70 or below. Third, states are allowed to require the claimant to prove his IQ by a preponderance of the evidence. The Court can then supply a durable rule of decision by which a state can adopt a reasonably reliable method for handling multiple IQ scores that, when it suggests the offender's IQ is greater than 70, his sentence does not violate Atkins. Here, every identified method of handling multiple IQ scores favors the conclusion that Smith is not intellectually disabled. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: How would you specifically handle the multiple scores here?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: In this case, the consistent evidence is that Smith's IQ is above 70, and we've offered five different ways to see that. But we think, ultimately, it's Smith's burden to come with a method that proves that his IQ could -- is likely below 70.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: So what did the -- is the -- is the test whether or not it is likely or is?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Likely below 70 is our understanding of the preponderance burden in Alabama. Other states are free to adopt other burdens of proof.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: And what was the finding of the court of appeal and the district court?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: The sentence "Smith's IQ is below 70" doesn't appear in the district court's opinion, nor in the court of appeals' opinion. They found the first prong satisfied by a preponderance of evidence but only after they changed the standard to be not just intellectual functioning but -- not just IQ but a broader conception of intellectual functioning.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: What if the scores were 69, 68, 69, 69, 75? I mean, would you concede in that case that it was -- the actual IQ is below 70, or would you still argue, well, 75 is above, so we've got to look at all sorts of other things?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Well, the -- the burden would still be on the offender to prove a likelihood that his IQ is 70 or below, and so he'd have to come with a method. Here, Smith didn't, so --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: He'd have to come with a what?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: With a method for assessing those scores. Here, Smith didn't, so his claim fails. We have an argument that the highest score should be given more probative weight because there are many ways that an IQ test can underestimate IQ, if the offender is distracted, fatigued, ill, or because of the incentive to avoid the death penalty, and --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, it does -- you can see why that might be regarded as a little results-oriented. When you have scores above 70, you want to average them and discount the -- the one below. But, when they're all below, you don't do that. You instead say you've got to look at all these other factors.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: It's not results-oriented, Your Honor, because there is a scientific warrant behind it. And we have to think about what IQ tests are doing. They're trying to measure the capacity for intellectual performance. And so someone's best score is the best representation of their capacity --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But, Mr. Overing, it seems to me that you are actually changing the standard. You've accused the district court and the court of appeals of doing so. But, in our case law, consistently, we've said that the first prong relates to a showing of significant sub-average general intellectual functioning. The words "IQ score under 70" does not appear. Those -- that's not the standard. I appreciate that that's one of the ways in which science has determined that you can make such a showing, but we also have allowed for evidence related to adaptive functioning to be taken into account when looking at intellectual functioning. So I think what you've done is shift this to be all about the IQ test in a way that is not supported by our case law.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Atkins cited the fourth edition of the DSM, and I think the phrase that you just used appears in -- in that edition and then further defines what general intellectual functioning means. And it used to mean an IQ of 70 or below. Before that, it was 65. Now they're saying it's 75. But IQ has always been the primary criterion by which intellectual --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The primary but not the sole, and we've never said it's been the sole. And what the district court did here was look not only at the IQ scores holistically but also other evidence of adaptive functioning. And that's precisely what our case law says that the courts are supposed to do. So -- so -- so let me -- let me ask you, why do you think that the test should be simply and solely IQ score cut off at 70?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: IQ is originally how intellectual disability was defined as a condition, and it's always been the primary criterion. And states are allowed to take the best evidence of intelligence and to make that the test. And --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But what -- what do -- what do -- what do we do with the brief of the American Association of Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities, the actual experts in this area, who say -- and I'm looking on page 24 here -- "Other than requiring the careful use of clinical judgment and professional responsibility in performing a complete analysis, there can be no single mandatory empirical method for clinicians to use in considering multiple scores or in determining whether or not someone is intellectually disabled." They look at a lot of things. So reducing this to a single score seems to me misguided.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Well, if there can be no one mandatory method, then it's up for states and their legislatures to decide. That brief also said on page 15 that IQ remains the best single representation of intellectual functioning. And the alternative is very unworkable. Courts do not have the means by which they can compare the evidence at prong 2, for example, that he struggles with navigating the subway system or doesn't know how to cook certain dishes against the objective IQ evidence. How many points --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Counsel, can I --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- can --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Oh.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: It's okay.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm having a really hard time with this case because I don't think you're wrong. States can define this the way they want. The problem here is not what the State of Alabama is doing. The Alabama state is doing what all courts are doing. You haven't pointed me to Perkins or any other court where the rule that you're proposing has been adopted by Alabama. When we get that case, we might answer your question, but, in the abstract, I don't know how we can answer it here. You didn't argue this below. Your own expert did exactly what you say is wrong. Your own expert said he had valid -- he had valid scores between 72 and 78 and he relied on all of the other indicia to determine that he believed this person wasn't intellectually disabled. Respond -- Respondent's experts concluded differently, and the district court credited Respondent's experts. But please point me to somewhere in the record in the district court where you made this argument. In 2015, the Eleventh Circuit, not the order before us, determined that the Alabama courts had erred unreasonably because they had done exactly what Hall told them they couldn't do. They looked at the three scores that were extant at the time, 72 -- I think it was 72 and two 74s or something like that. It was all -- it wasn't the 78, the 78 came later -- and they said on the basis of those scores alone he wasn't intellectually disabled. And that's exactly what Hall the year before had said was wrong to do. You didn't appeal that decision. So we have to accept that the district court is now the finder of fact. And if the district court is the finder of fact, it can do exactly what you're saying: Follow Alabama law. And show me one case in Alabama that has followed your rule. Tell me where in the trial record you argued that somehow aggregating scores, that somehow medium scores, that somehow there was one perfect IQ score that states could require. Just show it to me. Point me to the record.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Respectfully, Your Honor, at the start of your question, you asked what is Alabama law, and I say Perkins says it's an IQ of 70 or below. And you implied that that's --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But how do you prove that? And Alabama courts do exactly what the district court does here. It looks at the SEMs. That's what the court did here. It then decides whether the person is within the range of intellectual disability, which the court did here. The SEMs, certainly, at least three out of the five are within disability. And then it looks to other things to determine whether the person's intellectually disabled. It's exactly what we told people to do in Hall. It's exactly what we told people to do in Moore. What you're asking us to do is to undo those cases because both in Hall and in Moore there were various scores, just like in this case. In Hall, there was nine scores between -- what were the figures? Nine scores between -- I can't remember now.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Seventy-one and above.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Not much different. Even worse than here, between 71 and 80. Here, there are five scores between 72 and one score of 78. And in Hall, only one of the lowest scores fell within SEM.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: So I'd like --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And, here, there were three.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: I'd like to clarify first Alabama law because, in Your Honor's own description of what the state courts did in this case, they relied on the scores of 72, 74, and 75. And now they have Hall --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Without looking at the SEM. And we, in Hall, the year before said you have to look at the SEM.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: We -- we've cited --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And they didn't.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: We've cited multiple Alabama cases -- Bush, Byrd, and Callen -- all of which had similar scores to Smith's here.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: If you read every one of those cases, the score -- the court did -- the courts there did exactly what this court did here. They looked at all of those scores. Then they looked at the adaptive functioning to determine whether the person was actually at the lower end of the score or the higher end of the score.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: State courts have --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Can you dispute that, that that's what those courts did?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: In some cases, the courts do look to other evidence, but they don't grant an Atkins claim based on that other evidence. There's not an Alabama case in which an offender was known to have an IQ above 70 and yet won his Atkins claim. And there's certainly not an Alabama case in which the courts plucked the lowest score alone and said that represents a possibility of intellectual deficits and moved on.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: That's actually --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You're --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That's not what happened here.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: That's my question, counsel. I -- I wanted to know about the Alabama law too. Could you just precisely explain -- not -- I don't want to delve into the record in this case, so my question is a little bit than just -- different from Justice Sotomayor's. I just want to know what is the Alabama rule because it seems to me that Justice Sotomayor is right that there are some Alabama cases that do move on to adaptive functioning. And so what about the other indicia and what role do they play in Alabama law?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: I think, since Hall, courts have been confused in Alabama and nationwide about what some of the dicta in Hall means. I mean, with all due respect to this Court, there are some confusing lines in Hall to interpret and apply in these cases with multiple scores if Hall applies at all to cases with multiple scores. And so the Alabama courts are -- are grappling with that too, and they say: Well, we looked at everything. We looked at the whole record. But there is not a case in which that other evidence has ever trumped IQ in Alabama. And what Alabama wants to do is best exemplified by its pre-Hall case law, such as Ex Parte Smith, in which a 72 seriously undermined the Atkins claim.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: But, Mr. --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: But I'm confused because it doesn't seem like Alabama prohibits the court from going on. So, if a -- if a federal court is trying to figure out what the Alabama law is, wouldn't a federal court be justified, say, if we remanded it to the Eleventh Circuit in considering factors beyond the IQ scores?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Well, I -- I think the best evidence of what Alabama law is is how the courts applied it in this case. I think, at the end of the day, this Court has to determine what is the federal constitutional minimum, what's the floor for Atkins claims.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Counsel, isn't -- isn't -- I mean, with respect to Alabama and its law, whatever it may or may not be, can a federal habeas court grant relief without finding a violation of federal law or the federal Constitution, in this case, the Eighth Amendment?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: No. And -- and that matters quite a lot.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So what matters is, is he intellectually disabled. That's the federal law, right?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: The federal law is Atkins and Hall.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Exactly. That you can't execute an intellectually disabled person.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: What is your understanding of the role of state law in making that determination whether Atkins and Hall leave discretion to Alabama in defining what intellectually disabled is? That's my question. Obviously, it's an Eighth Amendment question, but what is the relationship between the Eighth Amendment and state law on this question?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: So, for Atkins, page 317 said that, to the extent there's disagreement, the states have disagreements about who qualifies, who is, in fact, intellectually disabled. And so there's some suggestion in Smith's brief that the state's discretion extends only to procedural or evidentiary matters. But I read that paragraph in Atkins to mean that states actually have leeway as well in defining intellectual disability. And the definitions change. One reason states need discretion is that the line used to be 67. AAMR at one point put the line at 85. Now it's 70 or maybe it's 75 or somewhat above 75. And so states are not required to divine from these different psychiatric manuals what the best possible science could be when they're making a legal determination of intellectual disability.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Let -- let me posit this to you, that Atkins was trying to give states a little leeway because Atkins was -- it was a -- it was a -- it was a strong new holding from this Court applying new ground and it tried to give states some leeway. But, at the end of the day, a federal habeas court cannot grant relief on the basis of a violation of state law.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Correct. States can be more lenient, but that would not be a ground for federal habeas relief. Smith has an Atkins claim.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: We have to find that there is an actual Eighth Amendment violation in this case.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Correct. Smith has an Atkins claim, not a Perkins claim. And Alabama is not more lenient than Atkins. Alabama is not more lenient than Hall. Alabama is trying to enforce the constitutional --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: It would be rather surprising to hear Alabama come in here and say we're more lenient --

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: -- than what the court is requiring us to do.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: It could happen, but it seems unlikely.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Not in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: And under Atkins, what -- what do you -- what do you think about -- because you -- I -- I'm sorry. My time's expired.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: No, please continue.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Asking about, you know, what the Eighth Amendment and what do we do with our precedents in this area. What if we said, under Atkins, states can implement prong 1 however they want, because that's what Atkins says, as long as they don't treat a single score in the low 70s as decisive, Hall, and, two, don't use extraneous facts to IQ scores to outweigh a low score, Moore?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: I think that's correct, with one caveat. It's not just a low score in the low 70s, but it has to be a score that has an error range and that could be one standard error of measurement according to the APA's brief in Hall on page 23 that includes 70 within its span. So that's how I would read Hall, but, yes, those two rules are -- are how we read the precedents.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas?

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: No.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Alito?

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: No.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You know, I don't see any state that defines intellectual disability this way. I certainly don't see -- or to prove it this way. I don't see anything in Alabama's law that suggests it's following that rule. You're making something wholesale up. And you're claiming that there's disagreement in the lower courts. I don't see all that much disagreement. In Smith's table on page 17 of the appendix, he has cases from across all the states. And although you try to reap some confusion about -- among them, they all seem to be following the method the district court here followed. And we announce a new rule. We've announced more confusion into an area where there's pretty standard procedures. They do exactly what the district court did here. I have no case that I have found, and I want you to find one state case that applies the rule as you've articulated it.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Byrd versus State clearly states that the offender has the burden to prove by a preponderance of the evidence that his IQ is 70 or below.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: There is no dispute about that. Assume I take that as a given. We didn't grant cert on that because that really wasn't disputed anywhere. So where's the rest of what you're saying, that states determine prong 1 based solely on IQ scores and some statistical unknown method of creating a medium composite score?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: There -- there are different methods. Some states -- some state courts have taken the average or the median, or they've simply said the scores are consistently above 70. And so it's the offender's burden to come back and overcome that with a statistical method. It's not Alabama's burden.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Tell me what -- name one court that has held that, that it's up to the defendant to prove the first prong by a preponderance of the evidence with some statistical measure. It's not what I see. I have not found one state case that has required that.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: It -- it --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And now you're going to put in a new requirement that's actually even more complex than what states have been doing.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Well, Hall itself said that evaluating multiple scores is a complicated endeavor. Experts are --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I am asking you for a simple answer. Name one state case that has made your prong 1 dependent on a statistical analysis of what's somehow either an aggregated, medium, composite, I don't care what word you use, one state case that has required that.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Well, I -- I think courts haven't needed to go that far because offenders haven't come with a method. They haven't satisfied their burden in these cases when this Court --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But we've -- they've granted -- they have granted relief to others without it.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Not in Alabama. Sometimes the Eleventh Circuit will do that -- applying this lowest-score rule. And there is a division of authority. The Eighth, Ninth, and Eleventh Circuits look at the lowest score alone and say that it --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: They -- they may well be wrong, and I think they probably -- I haven't looked at those cases carefully enough to say whether they made error or whether you're characterizing them in a way that's not quite accurate, like here. The Eleventh Circuit made clear, as did the district court, that it wasn't relying on just the one raw score. There were three scores that were within the SEM of 70 and below.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Hall said that IQ is a -- a measurement that has statistical properties which we can evaluate. And that's why we take into account the standard error of measurement. The experts in this case are also telling us --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Point to one case that has used -- required plaintiffs to come in with the statistical mathematical certainty of a particular number.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Not certainty but a preponderance of the evidence. And this --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan?

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Your answer must be --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan?

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Overing, I -- I guess, when I read Hall and Moore, I come away with a couple of things that are relevant here and then a lot of space for a state court to do what it wishes, including with respect to multiple scores. But here are the two things that I take from Hall and Moore that are relevant here. The first is that in looking at any given score, you have to take into account the standard error of measurement, right, so that a 72 with a standard error of measurement of five becomes a 67 to a 77. And that's one thing that they said, right? You can't just treat it as above 70 because you have to recognize that SEM. I don't think you disagree with that.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Except for the five. Correct, courts have to take into account standard error, but it's not always five.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Of course.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: I'm just using that as a hypothetical, all right?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: The second thing that it seems to me come out of those two cases is when you have a score, and most of these cases, you're going to have -- you know, almost all of them, you're going to have multiple scores, but when you have one score because, in both of these cases, they just treated that low score as dipping below the 70 or a 70 or below, that that gives the defendant the opportunity to come forward with adaptive functioning evidence. It's just an opportunity to come forward with that evidence. Now, at that point -- and this is true of a state that does the whole thing holistically. It's also true of a state that breaks it up into prongs. At that point, a court can decide how that evidence compares to all the other evidence in the case. And if it's a multiple score case where it's only one score that's dipping below 70, of course, the court can say, you know, there are five other scores that are above 70, and we think that that's highly probative. So, you know, it's very nice that he had the chance to come in with adaptive evidence, but that's not going to play in our analysis because we look at all the scores and the scores alone do it for us. I think a court is definitely allowed to say that after Hall and Moore as long as they've given a person the opportunity to come in with adaptive evidence. But they're also allowed to take into account that there are a lot of scores that suggest that the person is not intellectually disabled. So that's what it seems to me the law is coming out of these two cases. Do you agree with that or do you not agree with that?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: No, I don't agree with that because Hall didn't answer the question of what to do with multiple scores. And so, if it --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: It didn't. I mean, it -- and -- and because it didn't, that's why I say it really does leave a lot of discretion to a state court to decide how it wants to deal with the fact that there are a lot of scores on the plus 70 side and not a lot on the minus 70 side. That's totally within a state court's discretion to say there are a lot of scores here on the plus 70 side; that's dispositive for us as long as they've given a person -- this is the only requirement that seems to me that comes out of Hall and Moore. They have to give the person with the minus -- with the 70 or minus score the opportunity to come in with adaptive evidence suggesting the opposite.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Well, that would be an extension of Hall, and we've said that Hall should not be extended.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: I would have thought that that's just what it says.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: If -- if it's reasonable for a court to do that on a set of facts, then it should also be reasonable for a state legislature to say the true IQ line is 70 and that can be dispositive. So, if a court could do that with scores of 72, 75, 76, why couldn't a legislature do the same thing and say: No, we don't want to leave that door open because we don't trust trial --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: I think the --

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: -- trial judges --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Yeah, I think --

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: -- to do this well.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Yeah, but I think that is the difference, right, that Hall and Moore did say you have to give the person the opportunity. You have to put it in front of the judge for the judge to say, you know, this is a case where I'm not convinced -- you know, there are five scores, but they're all hovering around the 70 mark, you know, as compared to a case where there are five scores and most of them are nowhere near the 70 mark. So you have to give this person the opportunity once you have that low score to say maybe it's that low score that should be -- that is more reflective of the person's intellectual function.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: There's more we can do with IQ. And -- and the experts are coming in and agree with us that there are ways that you can combine the scores. And so the offender's at least got to do that and at least has to show a -- a likelihood below 70. If there's a case where it's truly indeterminate, and that's what Hall seemed to think, that a score of 71 is so uncertain standing alone, if there were a case that's truly indeterminate, then perhaps there could be other evidence --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: I -- I don't think --

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: -- of intellectual functioning, not adaptive behavior, certainly.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: I don't think that they thought it was indeterminate standing alone. I mean, both Hall and Moore are multiple score cases, and both were perfectly aware that they were multiple score cases and talked, as you said, about the difficulties of dealing with multiple scores. So they knew that they were talking about multiple score cases. And the requirement that they set up is still, if you have one score that brings you into the 70 minus range, that's enough so that you have to a little bit -- you -- you have to open the door to a person's evidence. Now that's not to say you have to accept that evidence for a moment if there are a whole bunch of scores going the opposite way.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: We know what happens in these cases is that courts don't weigh the IQ evidence. They -- they don't say, well, the IQ evidence is really strong for the state, let me weigh that against his inability to buy car insurance or to maintain a bank account or something like that. They simply move on from IQ. And so, once you open the door to this kind of balancing test, the second prong is really malleable, really amorphous, and we're picking up disparate facts about someone's life, self-reported or by biased reporters. And so that evidence is not disciplining the inquiry. It's not adding more rigor to figuring out what the intelligence is. And if it were, then the IQ specialists would simply make a better IQ test that accounts for things like that.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Okay. Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch? Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So what are the different permissible Eighth Amendment approaches a state can use when there are multiple scores? For example, in your reply brief, you articulate the -- if the median is above 70. Is that -- if the median's above 70, can the state say that's it?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay. And you don't think Hall and Moore require more -- more analysis in those cases? You think that's a safe harbor, so to speak?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Yes, states can adopt reasonably reliable methods. And the APA handbook says that the median is option 1.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay. And if the -- another approach you articulate is the overlap approach, the overlap among each scorer's error range. Same question there. If the overlap -- well, you're a little bit ambiguous about what the overlap needs to be to give you certainty. What -- you want to explain that? I know you think it's satisfied here, but what's the rule?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: So the idea is that when there's a cluster of scores, that we can take into account each score's error range and then lay them on top of each other and see which numbers the error ranges have in common. So the intuition there is that maybe his true IQ is somewhere in the middle.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: What if the bottom of the error -- error range is 69 or 70, of the overlap error range?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: I -- I don't think that's a problem for the state because the question is still the likelihood that his IQ is 70 or below. So simply having the number 69 in an error range, whether using the overlap or some other method, doesn't reflect a greater than 50 percent chance that his IQ is below 70.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But, in any event, in this case, you say the bottom of the overlap is 73, I believe, correct? At least that's what you say in the reply brief. You say 73 to 77.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: And I think that should be a 78, but that's on the most generous assumption about the error range being plus or minus five.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay. And is the composite score method another method that the state can use, a state can use and, in your view, stop there?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: When it's available.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And are there any other methods?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: The average is a rough approximation of the composite score.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, there's a lot of skepticism about the average being accurate.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Well, the author of the A -- one of the authors of the APA's amicus brief in this case, Dr. Schneider, said that the average is a rough approximation of the composite score. Even if the composite score is the theoretically best method, states are allowed to adopt other methods. The fifth method would be to look at the high score when the highest score's entire error range is over 70. And those are the five that we've discussed as reasonably reliable methods.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And why there -- this picks up on the Chief Justice's question. Why are you picking just the high score in that fifth approach?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: The high score --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: That seems, as -- as he said, results-oriented.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: The -- the high score is more probative because there are many ways that an IQ score can err downward and very few ways that a score can err upward. And so especially to take into account the obvious incentive to malinger in these cases, the lowest score should be viewed with greater suspicion. Oklahoma has adopted a rule like this and it has withstood federal review for many years.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Then, to follow up on Justice Kagan's question, what's the logic or the rationale or the sense behind not having a district court or a trial court or a state court have the ability in those circumstances to go on and look at more? Because I think what you were saying is each of those approaches are essentially a safe harbor. You win. The defendant can't meet his or her burden in those circumstances, you win. The state court can't go on and look at other things. That's, I think, what you're saying. What's the logic, the rationale, the sense behind that?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Well, the adaptive behavior prong is simply not as good as IQ, and so, once you start weighing that subjective and amorphous evidence against the objective evidence of IQ, we don't think it's within courts' capacities to do that well. Even if clinicians could somehow weigh the evidence of the ability to buy groceries against the IQ score of 78, I don't know how courts would do that. Adaptive behavior was added later. Originally, the diagnosis was just based on IQ. There's great disagreement among the experts about what adaptive behavior even means. Is it 10 or 11 domains or three different domains? And there's great disagreement about how to even measure it. There are hundreds of different scales, inventories, batteries for adaptive behavior. I think people have a misconception that these are just other standardized tests, but often it's not, and there's a lot of judgment that goes into picking them. So I'll just give one example in this this case, a -- a reading comprehension test. The courts below credited evidence that -- that Mr. Smith is barely literate, I think at Petition Appendix 22, and then he was administered a reading comprehension test that showed he reads at an 11th grade level, which is clearly inconsistent with intellectual disability, but the state courts focus on the evidence from grade school. They pluck out a different fact. And so this is not a really rigorous scientific test the way that IQ is, the fruit of a multi-century enterprise to figure out the best possible estimate of intelligence.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett? Justice Jackson?

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So I guess I'm trying to understand the basis for our being able to determine that the district court in this case erred, which is what I understand you to be asking us to do. So are -- are you saying that Alabama has selected any one of those five methods by legislation, for example, and the district court refused to apply it in its consideration?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: No, Alabama hasn't adopted one of those methods.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: All right. So you explored a number of possibilities with Justice Kavanaugh, none of which Alabama has actually adopted, so we can't say that the district court erred by not applying Alabama law in that sense. Did you ask the district court to apply any of those methods in its consideration of -- of this defendant's intellectual disability?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Well, it's the Petitioner's burden to come in --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: No, no, no. Did -- your -- your burden right now as Petitioner is to establish that the district court erred, and so what I'm trying to ask you is whether you're saying that the district court erred by not evaluating this defendant in a way that he was never asked to do by the state below.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Well, it's a legal error because my answers to Justice Kavanaugh were that those are all reasonably reliable methods that the state could adopt. And if that's true, then it doesn't matter whether Alabama has, in fact, adopted them in statute because there's no Eighth Amendment violation.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That was my first question. Doesn't it matter for the purpose of our evaluation of the district court's error whether or not the district court was asked to evaluate this defendant using the method that you now say it was erroneous for the district court to not have used?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: The --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I mean, this never came up during -- like, no one said to the district court, please evaluate him using this method, and the district court said no. In that situation, we then assess whether the district court was wrong in refusing to adopt that method.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: We didn't get that far because we had five IQ scores that clearly show he loses, and then the district court focused only on the 72. And so the question --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But the district court was weighing all of the evidence under the standard that it seems like your expert also agreed was the right legal test at the time. And now you're saying the test should have been different. The district court was only allowed to look at one of these other five reliable methods, although no one said to the district court at the time, please look at that method.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: We said to the district court Alabama law is a true IQ of 70 or below by a preponderance. And then the district court did not apply that burden.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Okay. Let me ask you this because I do think that there's something about party presentation here that's a little troublesome. A couple weeks ago, in a case called Clark versus Sweeney, we summarily reversed a grant of habeas because the court of appeals had granted habeas based on an argument that the petitioner had not presented -- had not presented. So I feel like you're actually asking us to violate that same principle. You never asked the court of appeals in this case, for example, to evaluate the district court on the basis of these five methods and say that it was erroneous on that basis or on the cumulative -- on the cumulative effects test. I'm -- I'm a little confused all around because I thought the question presented in this case was about the cumulative effects test and that that's what you were actually advocating for. But that didn't come up in the lower courts. So how could we possibly reverse on the basis of these kinds of arguments that were not presented below?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: We absolutely did present evidence from Dr. King at JA 270 and 271 that his consistent scores were above 70. We didn't have to get to the level of granularity of specifying a particular method because there is no method in this case that can prove his IQ is likely 70 or below. We're offering those to answer the Court's question how to assess multiple IQ scores.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And your -- your answer is not --

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: But it's sufficient to reverse.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: -- your answer is not something about cumulative effects is what the court had to do and didn't do? I -- I thought that was the test that you were requiring us to consider as the thing that a district court has to do.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Our first argument is that it didn't do that because it looked only at the possibility. And if the Court agrees --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: It didn't -- wait.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: -- it has to do that --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I'm sorry. I'm just -- I thought you were saying the district court erred because it didn't consider the cumulative effect of multiple IQ scores and that the cumulative effect was a thing that the district court didn't do. All I'm asking here is, did you ask the district court to do that, or did you say to the court of appeals the district court has erred because it didn't look at the "cumulative effect" of the multiple IQ scores?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Yes, we said that in the district court. With Dr. King's testimony, we said it, and --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Cumulative effect, I'll find it in the record?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: We -- we -- we --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Because I didn't see the words "cumulative effect" in the record.

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Well, there are lots of synonyms, but consistent scores above 70, that's the -- the same thing. And then, in the Eleventh Circuit, we presented this evidence and said the district court didn't take seriously the whole of the IQ evidence because it didn't even mention his score of 78, so it couldn't have done the cumulative effect. That's enough to reverse. If the Court wants to go further and discuss the reasonably reliable methods, we think that would be a good idea, especially for guidance for --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So you're not asking necessarily for that rule about the reasonably reliable methods? That certainly did not come up before?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: Well, that is the question presented, and so that would be a complete answer to Smith's claim, is that there is no way that he can prove an IQ below 70 because, even on these common and expert-approved methods, he hasn't shown that his IQ is below 70.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Graver. SUPPORTING THE PETITIONER

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Under Atkins, states have significant discretion in defining what it means to be intellectually disabled and what a defendant must do to prove it. Alabama has exercised that discretion to require that Respondent show his true IQ is likely 70 or below based on all relevant evidence, including his multiple IQ scores all above 70. The courts below erred because they replaced that burden with some variant of a one-low-score rule. Everyone now agrees that such a rule is wrong. Instead, Respondent focuses on justifying the opinions below as applying a holistic analysis. But that misses the point. The problem here is not that the courts below simply looked at other evidence. It is that they measured that evidence against the wrong standard, and it's that they looked at that evidence on only one side of the scale, never considering the collective weight of Respondent's multiple IQ scores taken together. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: What exactly did Atkins say the Eighth Amendment violation was?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I think Atkins laid down a general rule.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Which -- which is?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Which is that you cannot execute the intellectually disabled, and the meaning of intellectual disability is something that states define with significant discretion.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: How did Atkins -- what did Atkins say about determining what intellectual disability meant? Actually, it used the word "retardation."

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: What did it say that was?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I think that Atkins looked -- and the Chief Justice put this well in Moore -- it's that the scope of Atkins reaches no further than the consensus of the states. So I think that to the extent there's content to that command, it's going to come from state consensus. But I really do think that Atkins largely, much as this Court has done in the insanity context, outsourced the definition of "intellectual ability" to the states.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, I'd like to -- you -- you're focusing in on Atkins, but Hall and Moore say something very different than what you're saying. In Hall, Mr. Hall had scores between -- nine scores over 40 years. The lower courts found a score of 71 and 80, only two of the scores, to be valid. But Florida had a strict cutoff rule that said anyone with higher than a 70 could not be intellectually disabled. So you can't have a flat rule that says you get one score over 70, you're not -- you're not disabled, correct?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Correct. I think that just -- yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And what Atkins also said, I'm quoting, "For professionals to diagnose and for the law then to determine whether an intelligence disability exists once an SMN applies and the individual's IQ scores is 75 or below, the inquiry would consider factors indicating whether the person had deficits in adaptive functioning. Courts must analyze the STEM, and if the STEM includes 70, the defendant must be able to present additional evidence of intellectual disability, including testimony regarding adaptive functions." So for us to say what Petitioner wants would disavow that language in Hall. It would disavow nearly the identical language in Moore. In Moore, he had two valid scores of 74 and 78. Nowhere did we suggest that courts had to aggregate, cumulate, medium them out, composite them, do anything else with them. What we had -- and, there, the court held that the state courts had violated Hall when it discounted the lower end of Moore's STEM because he was malingering. The tests were valid. It had a SEM. The SEM was below 70. And then the Court held, once the SEM in some of those valid tests were below 70s, the courts "must continue the inquiry and consider other evidence of" -- "of intellectual disability where an individual's IQ score adjusted for the test's standard error falls within the clinically established range for intellectually functioning deficits." So you are asking us to tell courts below to do something substantially different than what we told them to do in Hall and Moore.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Respectfully, no, Justice Sotomayor. There's a big difference between saying you can move on and look at other evidence and saying you can then forget about IQ entirely.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Ah.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: The big problem here --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Okay. That's something different. What you're talking about then is not an -- a legal error by the courts below. You're talking about a different -- your difference of opinion.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: No, it is a legal error twice over, and I think it's a helpful thing to spell out here, is that even if you look at other evidence, you still need to circle back and see how that weighs against the evidence on the other side of the scale. And in this context, that is going to be the multiple consistent IQ scores together.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, wait a --

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: The two legal errors --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- minute. But that's what the district -- that's what the two experts -- the three experts here did. There was Dr. King, I think one was Dr. Chudy, who was the original expert, and then there was the evidentiary hearing, Dr. Fabian, if I'm not mistaken. Each one of them did exactly that. Your Dr. King looked at the scores and said, I'm looking at the functioning and I don't think he's on the lower end of 7 -- of 70 because, when I speak to him, he speaks well. He can respond. I think he functions at a higher rate. The other doctors, which the district court credited and did many, many more tests than Dr. King did, looked at all of the tests, all of the results, and said I'm convinced that his range is 70 and below.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: So, before turning to the experts, let me just emphasize what I think are two discrete legal errors with the way in which the courts thought about this under Alabama law. Even if you tend to look at the secondary evidence, you still need to circle back --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Are you saying it's wrong to do it? Do you say Alabama says it's wrong to do that?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Wrong to do what?

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Look at secondary evidence.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I don't think there's any per se rule in Alabama that says you cannot look at this. But the difference is --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So there's no per se --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Can you tell us what the two --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Can you please tell us what the two legal errors are?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Graver.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: So the basic idea is, where you have all of this evidence together, are you measuring it against the right standard? So one legal error that happened below is that the courts swapped essentially a preponderance burden with a possibility burden. But the other part is, even putting that to the side, the difficulty is that you still need to figure out what the ultimate question Alabama law is asking here, which is, is this other evidence strong enough to drag down the collective weight of IQ --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: So that seems --

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: -- below the line.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: -- totally right to me. So I'm -- I'm wondering -- you -- you heard the question that I asked Mr. Overing, and I'm wondering if we disagree at all. If I say the thing that Hall and Moore require is that when you have a score that's 70 or below, you do have to give the defendant an opportunity to come in and to talk about adaptive functioning. But, having given the defendant the opportunity to talk about adaptive functioning, yes, of course, you can go back to the scores and to the fact that there are a lot of scores on one side, the upper side of 70, that make you think that that evidence of adaptive functioning just cannot possibly carry the day. Is that what you're saying, or are you saying something more than that?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I think I agree with 95 percent or at least with one or two asterisks. And let me say how I think that cashed out here and then what the constitutional rule is for how you just think about IQ. The issue is that what you were describing before just did not happen here. The only time that the courts grappled with --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Okay. So I -- I take that point.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Let's -- let's presume that I don't care about it for the moment.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Fair enough.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: You know, that all -- all I'm trying to do is to figure out the rule, and then we can go back and say whether the court did it or not do it.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: So I think that the part where I disagreed with what you said before is just in the sense that a single low score in all circumstances, no matter what, will always preclude a state from being able to focus on IQ. So imagine, for instance, just a case --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: No, I -- I don't think it would -- it would -- the only thing that I think a -- a single low score does is to say, yes, at that point, the defendant does have to have the opportunity to come in with adaptive evidence. And I take Hall and Moore to have -- that is their holding.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Right. So I think I take it slightly less categorically than you do, which is just to say, so you imagine a case of, you know, 10 85s and one 72. The collective error range of those scores, even if you look at the 95 percent confidence interval, is not going to grace 70 or below. So imagine a situation like that where -- you -- I think you can structure it where the possibility remains so low --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Okay. So that's like the hypothetical that you gave in your brief, like if there are a lot of 90s, and I kind of take that point, right, because, like, at some point, there's no clinician in the world who's going to say that just because you have, like, one outlier score, that -- that there's a possibility, right, if you have, like, six 90s and a 72. But, if you have something that's more along the lines of here --

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: -- right, then I take it that we are in agreement, tell me if I'm not, that at that point, the Hall and Moore ruling is you have to allow the person to come in with adaptive functioning, evidence but, of course -- may I, Mr. Chief Justice?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: But, of course, you can say this is outweighed by the fact that there's a multitude of scores on one side.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Yeah, I think that there is broad -- and if I can just stress the two key points of agreement is that moving on doesn't mean you're done. It involves circling back to the standard that state law imposes. And I think, in turn with that, it's helpful to clarify what I think is now a point of agreement, that the one-low-score rule reading of Hall and Moore where prong 1 is satisfied substantively so long as you have one low score is wrong.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas? Justice Alito?

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Let me see if I understand the colloquy you just had with Justice Kagan and -- and the degree to which you agree with what she said. Did either Hall or Moore hold anything with respect to how a case must be treated when there are multiple scores? Hall and Moore talk about one score and they say that dealing with multiple scores is a complicated matter. But do they say anything about how a court is to deal with a situation where there are more than one scores?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: No, not expressly.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: So, if there is a case with one score and the one -- the -- the standard error of measurement has a range that is below 70 and that's it, that's all that's in the case, one score, standard error of measurement is below 70, yes, they -- Hall says that allows the -- the -- the -- the prisoner to proceed with evidence about adaptive functions, but it doesn't say how the Court is to deal with a situation where there are multiple scores.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: That's correct. And this is what I was trying to get at before, is that I do think in a lot of the solutions that I think might come with concerns that come with looking at injecting adaptive functioning into this space is I do think states have some discretion about when you want to sort of focus on IQ alone as a threshold matter. And I do think, to the extent that a defendant or -- just can't come in and show that your multiple scores have any realistic possibility of dipping below any concerning range, that is a time where a state can say we don't want to inject any of this other stuff into it, just focus on IQ.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Okay. So what we are looking for here under our precedents is the evolving standard of decency that exists on this issue about how to -- how to decide whether a -- someone who's sentenced to death has significantly sub-average intellectual functioning. In prior cases where we have tried to determine what the national consensus is, we have looked at state laws, things that are enacted by the state legislature on the theory that they reflect the moral judgment of the people of that state as expressed through their elected representatives. But what do we do with a situation -- and let's say that if state -- a state made the law through case law without being -- without thinking that it had to do something one way or the other because of our decisions about constitutional standards. Put that aside. What do we do with a situation like we have here, where state courts and lower federal courts are trying to figure out what we said in -- what Hall and Moore mean? And so their decisions reflect their interpretations, perhaps erroneous interpretations, of those two precedents of this Court. Do we -- do we look at those to see whether there's a national consensus?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: No, I don't think so. I think there's a difference between -- the idea for a national consensus is when a certain moral judgment has the imprimatur of the people. I don't think that happens when a court imposes that standard on an incorrect understanding of federal law. So, to the extent that this Court is still doing head-counting for purposes of Eighth Amendment, I don't think that courts misunderstanding Hall or Moore would factor in favor for whatever new rule is being proposed.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Now I don't know whether you've surveyed the state -- state statutes, so perhaps you're not in a position to answer this question, but if one were to survey the state -- the statutes of -- of all the states that have the death penalty and see whether they have adopted one approach or another approach to the situation we're dealing with here, what would we find?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: So I don't -- I -- I can't speak to the legislatures just because I don't know what was motivating them. Alabama was able to catalogue, I think, a few examples in their brief of instances where state courts were applying what is essentially the one-low- score rule. That erroneous understanding of Hall and Moore, which, again, we all agree is an erroneous understanding of Hall and Moore, I don't think that that counts as a moral endorsement for the one-low-score rule.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: And Justice Kavanaugh asked your friend about the advantages and disadvantages of a safe harbor as opposed to the alternative, and maybe you would -- I'd be interested in what you have to say about that. If there's a -- a safe harbor in the sense that a -- a person sentenced to death who's claiming a intellectual disability has to meet some concrete standard before the case can go on, will there not be greater consistency and predictability, and is that not one of the -- the lodestars of the Court's death penalty jurisprudence, as opposed to a situation where everything is up for grabs in -- in every case and both sides can bring in experts to testify about the person's intellectual disability or lack of intellectual disability and every trier of fact is going to decide that on an individualized basis?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: So I a hundred percent agree with that, Justice Alito. And if I can just emphasize one point because I don't think there's any practical daylight between what I'm saying and what Alabama was just talking about. I think just -- the point I was making before is there might not be a per se legal bar in looking at the other evidence, but the IQ evidence may nonetheless be practically dispositive in a given case. So what I'm understanding essentially what my friend was saying is, well, if we look at the IQ scores, it was the Defendant, the Respondent's burden to essentially show at step 1 that the error range of those scores together reaches 70 or below because, even if -- if that range never reaches 70 or below in the first place, there's no realistic possibility that any of the secondary evidence on the other side of the scale is going to drag you over. So I think that that's just the -- that's more of a practical matter with the facts of this case, where IQ happens to be dispositive just because there's not going to be anything on the other side of the scale that tips it over.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm not sure how that happens. There were nine scores in Hall between 71 and 80, and we still sent it back for the court to consider additional evidence, including adaptive functioning, correct? What Justice Alito was saying is states could do something like he suggests, but is that constitutionally required?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I think the difference, though, is --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Answer my question. Would states have to do it that way?

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Or could they do it now in whatever way they thought, one of the five ways suggested by the other side or the way they're actually doing it now?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: The answer is uncertain for the reasons I was explaining with Justice Kagan. And I can't -- you know, I'm not good enough at math to do this on the fly, but the basic idea is that if those collective scores do not produce a range that even grades at 70 --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Has any state adopted that measure?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: -- I don't think there's any constitutional bar --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Has any state approached the issue that way with multiple scores?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I don't think so. I think our big --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Did Dr. King approach it that way below?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: In part. I think he focused on IQ and said that it did not fall below 70 and then also said that the other evidence on the side -- on the other side of the ledger wasn't strong enough to show any other form of disability either.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: What I thought he did was exactly what all the experts did, is three of the five fell within intellectual disability, and then he relied on adaptive functioning to say he thought he was not intellectually disabled.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Let me make one quick point --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: -- just in addition to this, is that what you won't find in any of the expert reports is a conclusion with respect to what Alabama law asked. None of them found that Respondent's true IQ was likely 70 or below. So finding that Respondent did not satisfy his state law burden --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry, who didn't find that?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: No expert in this case -- you won't find any --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Wait a minute. That's what they did say.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: No, no, it's -- it's a --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: That two of them -- at least two of Respondent's experts found he was intellectually disabled. That was the finding the district court made.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: It depends what definition you're using. The experts were using clinical definitions that are distinct from Alabama law.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I don't --

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: No one made --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- I don't --

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- disagree with you, but the district court was following Alabama law. It's -- these things are not statutory. Most of these -- the very few, maybe Oklahoma is now, where one score above 76 disqualifies you. I don't know how that's not different than the 70, one score above 70 situation might be, but we'll look at Oklahoma when it comes to us.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Sure. The only thing I'll add is that I don't think the district court was following Alabama law because, as my friend emphasized, I think a very telling point is that you will not find a single sentence in anything the district court wrote that said Respondent's IQ is likely 70 or below. We think that they were laboring over --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Oh, is that what you think is -- they did wrong?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I think that there's -- what I was trying to get at at the start is there's two problems. They misunderstood the standard for a possibility idea from the one-low-score rule.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: That was only the Eleventh Circuit who said that. The district court certainly didn't say that.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: But then the basic part too is returning to what Alabama law is, which is a numerical inquiry, does the evidence show that you've been able to drag down the IQ scores below 70. There's no engagement with that. The basic I --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But that -- that was never argued by Alabama.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Oh, no, it -- it was one of their leading arguments, which was that you did not consider the totality of the evidence. And when you're trying to figure out whether the secondary evidence drags --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, that --

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: -- the IQ down --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- that -- that's -- all right. I'll stop.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But that's attributing to one broad statement a specific argument that I don't see. Where did they say that the adaptive functioning should be ignored because the cumulative effect of the scores showed he wasn't --

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: It's two sides of the same coin. What it's saying is there's no need to move on to other stuff because, here, Respondent hasn't even carried his initial burden of showing it will do any good.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you. Justice Kagan? Justice Gorsuch?

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: A few questions there where I don't think you got to finish your answer. Here's your shot.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I would be pretending if I remember what any of those questions were.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: So, you know, I think, instead of, you know, turning myself in a circle, if there's anything, you know, that you wish I'd spoke to --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, no, no. Well, no, there isn't. I want to get what your argument is as I'm here to understand it.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Not -- not -- not -- not anything else. And I think one of the questions was what do you think the error was that the district court made and that the Eleventh Circuit made? I think that was one question that I'd like to hear the answer to.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Right. So I think there's two related errors. And the first is what we were saying before, is that they just misunderstood what the substantive standard is. I'm going to look at this evidence, I'm going to ask whether there's a possibility. Alabama looks for a likelihood. So that's mistake number one. But the second part is, even if you say it's applying the right standard, there's still a basic analytical error because, once -- if you look at the opinions below, once the courts consider multiple IQ and conclude that it's theoretically possible Respondent's IQ dips to 70 or below, IQ falls out of the case entirely. And that's a basic analytical error because, of course, not all possibilities are the same. A defendant with one score of 75 is going to have a really different burden than a defendant with a hundred scores of 75. So the analysis here, what it should have looked like is taking account of that dynamic, which is not just saying, well, there's a possibility IQ falls away, I'm going to look at this secondary evidence. You then needed to circle back and see if that secondary evidence was strong enough to drag down the IQ scores below the line set by Alabama law.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: And the first error, though, might be itself dispositive, it sounds like.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Yeah, they're independent errors.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Either one of which is sufficient?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay. And you agree that at the end of the day, before a federal court can grant habeas relief, it has to find a violation of the Eighth Amendment here?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Yes. Can I add one piece to it, is that --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: If you must.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: -- the -- the wrinkle here which I think is a bit difficult is the innovation of Atkins --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: -- which is to --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Innovation. That was the word I was looking for.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: -- which is to identify a federal rule, a general rule against executing the intellectually disabled but largely outsourcing its definition to state law.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: At -- at -- at bottom, though, I agree with that. That's an innovation for sure. But it was trying to give some breathing room to states.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: There has to be a floor, an Eighth Amendment floor, beneath all that breathing room, right?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Presumably. And before a federal habeas court can grant relief, I would have thought you have to find a violation of federal law.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Yeah, I think that -- you know, make of it what you will, but I think the -- the -- the formal way in which it would work is there's a violation of Atkins, federal, because, one way or another, either met or did not meet or not even -- whether or not you satisfied Atkins turns on whether you satisfied state law.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Even if state law is well above the Eighth Amendment floor?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Oh, I understand. In that, I think that the answer would be probably yes, although I think it's difficult.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Really? Is it that difficult?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Well, I think that states have broad discretion to set -- I do think that at some point -- what I will say -- you know, let me take that back just a little bit in that --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: I would think that you might want to.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Yeah. That states are free to be more protective --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: -- than what the Eighth Amendment requires.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: How many times have we held that, right?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: A hundred percent. So, for instance, what I, you know, meant to say is imagine a state adopted the one-low-score rule as a matter of state law. Whether or not you violated that wouldn't necessarily implicate the Eighth Amendment because it's more protective than what the Eighth Amendment would require.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Exactly. And so --

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: -- before a federal habeas court could grant relief, it would still have to go beyond that and find a violation of the Eighth Amendment.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay. I would -- I'm glad we got to that. Okay. And then what do you think about my -- the -- the holding that I suggested to your friend from Alabama, you know, looking at what Hall and Moore and trying to figure out, because I agree there is some confusion and that's why we took the case, the QP we did, was trying to sort some of that out, that under Atkins, states can implement the first prong however they want as long as they don't treat a single low score -- a single score in the low 70s as decisive, Hall, or use facts extraneous to IQ scores to outweigh a low score, Moore. Is that a correct and -- and fair summary of the law that would provide guidance, do you think?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: A hundred percent.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: On the -- on the constitutional floor that you were just exploring, I want to ask the questions to you that I was exploring with Alabama's counsel. If the median is above 70, I understood them to say that you -- the defendant cannot make out a successful Eighth Amendment claim even if they might have something under state law. Do you agree with that?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I don't think that the Eighth Amendment necessarily speaks to that one way or another. I mean, again, I think --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So you don't agree with that then?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Well, I think that the standard for what the Eighth Amendment is at least under Atkins is going to only -- the -- the Eighth Amendment strictures are only going to reach as far as a nationwide consensus mandates. So, when it comes to those specific issues, to the extent there's no nationwide consensus on the question, no, I don't think you run afoul of that.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So what happens when the median is above 70 or when the -- I assume you're going to have the same answer on the overlap as well. So the overlap approach, let's say, as here, 73 to 77, they're saying, when that's the case, you cannot make out a successful Eighth Amendment claim, period.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I think that -- right.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Do you agree with -- do you agree with him on that?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: So I agree that it's practically dispositive under Alabama law.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Yeah, you said that. I -- I wrote that down, practically dispositive. What does practically as opposed to legally dispositive mean?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Because I think that it's not to say that -- that there's some per se legal error to moving on to other evidence in light of the source --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Could the other evidence then, when you move on, if you're saying they can move on or over the state's objection, you're saying the other evidence in some circumstances could outweigh the scores even when the median's above 70 --

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: This is -- yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Let me finish -- or when the range is something like 73 to 77, in those circumstances, unlike the state, you're saying the other evidence could outweigh in some cases?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I'm not saying it could outweigh. I think there's a difference between a constitutional rule as to whether or not you need to consider it, but I think, in a circumstance where you have consistent scores that are in the high 70s, this is what I mean by practically dispositive.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, if you're saying that you could consider it, but it's not dispositive like the state says, then I'm trying -- is this just for --

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I think this --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- going through the motions or what -- what --

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I think this is a product of -- again, this is going to be a product of state experimentation. If you have a totality-of-the-circumstances inquiry in some measure, it doesn't mean all the evidence is equally important. So, even if you decide to look at other evidence for one reason or another, the IQ scores might be so strong that there's no realistic possibility that that other evidence is going to drag the IQ scores down.
