turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: If the -- and I'll ask it again. If the median's above 70, the Court -- the state says end there, you're saying no, you can go on to consider the other evidence, the defendant can have you go on to consider the other evidence. In some cases, can the other evidence outweigh the scores even when the median is above 70?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: So, yeah, I can imagine a circumstance hypothetically where -- so suppose you have --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So that's a yes. That's -- okay, that's different from the state.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Again, I think that, you know, so imagine a circumstance where you have two scores of 71 and then you come in and essentially have this -- you have an expert say, well, the content of those IQ exams tested A, B, and C aspects of intellectual functioning. I have all of these, you know, neuropsychological batteries that test other aspects of intellectual functioning and they're really, really low. I could imagine that being, you know, in a state that has decided to adopt the totality-of-the-circumstances inquiry, that would be a kind of circumstance where, yes, the other evidence could put you below the line.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So I think it would be constitutional error then for a court to say the median is above 70 -- is 73, that's the end of it, you can't prevail on an Eighth Amendment claim. That would be constitutional error?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I would be -- so I think that I would be reluctant to phrase this in terms of median and composite, whatever it might be. And this is what I was trying to get at a little bit with Justice Kagan, is that there's some point in Hall I think blesses this practice. There's some point where the possibility of your scores dipping below -- dipping to 70 or below is remote enough that you can rely on IQ exclusively. I don't think this Court's precedents paints with a bright line. It certainly doesn't mandate at this point a specific median number, but I think that's the nature of the inquiry, is that with some --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But that's where you differ from the state. The state really was pretty definitive on that and you're not, which is fine. I'm just trying to explore this because we're going to have to dig through a lot in this case to explore the difference.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Right. I'll -- I'll let -- but, again, this is, you know, returning to the part of is it a real-world matter, I don't think it's going to make any difference here with the idea of --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Here meaning in this case?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Okay. That's all I need. Thank you.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Mr. Graver --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett?

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Mr. Graver, I just want to clarify. It seemed to me that what you were telling Justice Kavanaugh might have been different than what you said to Justice Gorsuch, and I want to make sure that I didn't misunderstand that. When Justice Gorsuch read you his proposed rule statement, it didn't include anything about necessarily having to consider the evidence of adaptive functioning. But I understood you to tell Justice Kavanaugh that you would have to go on to consider such evidence and that in some circumstances, it could outweigh the IQ scores even if they were in, say, the, you know, 73 to 78 range. Is there a difference, or am I misunderstanding this?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: I think a little bit. The part that I would want to focus on is the adaptive functioning piece, is that there's some aspect, I think, as we were getting at, there's some rule of Hall, taking Hall at face value, that I -- at some point, IQ scores alone can't be the only thing you're looking at. I do think states have some discretion after that to structure what the other evidence is. And I would imagine that a rule that, for instance, focuses just on conventional evidence of intellectual functioning would be constitutional. The way in which that -- if a -- if a state imposed that substantive standard, the question that would follow is, well, are Hall's references to adaptive functioning, that dicta itself, is that something this Court wants to give the force of law that you need to consider this bucket of evidence in particular. I don't see any constitutional mandate to do that. But I think the only point is is that so long as your scores touch a certain level, perhaps IQ can't be the only thing you're considering at prong 1.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson?

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I have to say I think that the arguments and assertions in this case are all over the map and very hard to follow, so I'm trying -- trying to understand how and to what extent the district court erred in this case given the law as it existed at the time the district court ruled as opposed to the law that Alabama wishes it had enacted or the cases that it wishes it had already put into place with respect to standards that it's coming up with now. So I understood you to say that there were two errors, something about likelihood or possibility -- versus possibility, and the second is one -- the one I'm most focused on, which is that under Alabama law, you understand that the law is and was at the time of the district court that if you're looking at secondary evidence, was the secondary evidence strong enough to drag down what you had gleaned previously from the IQ scores. Is that -- is that what you understood Alabama law to be and, therefore, it was an error for the district court not to apply it?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Yeah, and I don't think it's an IQ-specific insight. I think it's just a common-sense point about the nature of how preponderance would work.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I just want to know if it existed as a standard so that we can say the district court erred.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Yeah. I think that when you need to show that some -- a fact is more likely than not in light of the totality of the evidence, you can't then exclude the most probative evidence as part of that weighing. So the issue here that the district court did is it allow -- it said that the IQ scores allowed it to move on to other evidence, but then it forgot about the first half of the scale. It never looked at the collective weight of the IQ scores all above 50 to see --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But it did -- it did entertain expert testimony. And this is the second part of my question to you, is I'm trying to understand, there is a nub of this that's a question of fact, right? Is this person intellectually disabled? And I don't take it that you disagree that experts are going to be involved somehow in making this assessment, right?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: They're not only testing, but they're also looking at other evidence to the extent courts allow them to. So what I understood happened in this case is we had a series of experts come in, evaluate not only the test scores but also the other evidence, and testify as to whether or not they thought this person had an intellectual disability. Now I appreciate that the state's expert said no, but the other experts said yes, and the court looked at that and made a decision. I don't understand why that's error from the court's perspective when we -- we -- we ordinarily allow for hearings with evidence and testimony that goes to issues of fact and the court decides.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Right. So I don't -- I wouldn't think about this as a battle of the experts cases because --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But why not? We had experts --

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: -- who testified to this issue.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: That's the part where I disagree with you. No expert testified to whether or not Respondent satisfied his burden at prong 1 as understood by Alabama law. All of the evidence about intellectual functioning was measured against clinical definitions that are different in kind from what Alabama asked. So, again, the experts offered in their insights --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Okay. Can you point to a case in which Alabama says, Experts, you can only testify for the purpose of our law to the standard as we are now articulating it? Because, to the extent that Alabama sat there and heard the experts testifying and didn't object on the basis that you're saying or didn't argue to the district court there's a problem with this testimony because he's testifying to things that Alabama law doesn't consider, I think you may have waived this.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: No, I -- I think just the basic point is that it's not an all-or-nothing inquiry either way.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I'm just trying to understand what was argued below. If you're now saying the problem with the -- with the testimony before the district court of the experts is that it wasn't focused in the way that Alabama law requires, where's the objection from Alabama below saying that?

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: The fact that it doesn't answer the entire legal analysis does -- or legal question before the district court does not mean the expert testimony is not relevant. But the district court then needs to take that evidence, put it into the equation as structured by Alabama law. So what I mean by that is that it is true that the expert said, well, this secondary evidence has a bit of a downward pull, but that is the --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The expert did say? You --

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: But that is the start, not the end of the analysis. And that's the key problem with the district court, is that it never looked back to see the weight of the thing that it is pulling because, essentially, what it said is this downward pull is dispositive. It never returned to the collective weight --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: MR. GRAVER
turns.text: -- of the IQ scores as part of that analysis.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Waxman.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Just to clarify one thing, the statutory standard in Alabama for prong 1 is substantially sub-average intellectual functioning. Now courts sometimes use the term, improperly so, "true IQ of 70 or below." But every court in Alabama and this Court and every other court in every other state that I am aware of understands that raw observed test scores is not the definition of true IQ. Now, in deciding whether a defendant has carried his burden to prove that he suffers from substantially sub-average intellectual functioning, Alabama law, like near -- like that of nearly all other states, all other states with the qualified sometimes exception of Oklahoma, requires courts to evaluate all probative evidence as to intellectual functioning offered by either side. That includes evidence of intellectual functioning other than IQ test scores at least where a court, considering expert testimony, concludes that those scores alone don't decide the issue. The courts below did exactly that by evaluating evidence of Smith's significant impairment on other neuropsychological tests of intelligence -- this is pages 88 and 90 to 92 of the Petition Appendix -- his grade school records, which showed that on every measure he was two to four years below grade average, culminating in a diagnosis of mental retardation in the seventh grade. The -- sorry. The warden and the Solicitor General ask you to reverse the lower court's faithful application of Alabama law. And I am holding all 12 decided, reported cases on this issue post-Hall in Alabama, and there is not a single case, including the two that my friend mentioned, in which the court said we're only going to look at the test scores, we are not going to consider other evidence. In Perkins itself, which is the lead Supreme Court case, there -- I'm sorry.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Before you go on to the -- another granular argument --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I'm not leaving.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: -- what is precisely in -- to your understanding, is Alabama law?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Alabama law --

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Just -- just -- just the pure law, not this case. What does it require?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Alabama law requires that in evaluating whether an individual is intellectually disabled, three prongs must be met, substantially sub-average intellectual functioning, substantially sub-average adaptive functioning, and onset before the age of 18. And the -- the Alabama Supreme Court in Smith versus State specifically said and subsequently repeated that Al -- the Alabama legislature has adopted the most liberal definition of "intellectual disability" for purposes --

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: -- of the Eighth amendment.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: So the -- excuse me. But do you agree that compliance with Alabama law would be compliance with the Eighth Amendment?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: In this instance, yes. I could imagine a situation in which, you know, Alabama said we're just never going to execute anybody who gets a score --

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Well, let me just put it a different way. Do you think that the man -- that the statement or, in theory, the pure Alabama law, not in application here, is enough to comply with the Eighth Amendment?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: In this instance -- given the way that Alabama has defined "intellectual disability," yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: In other words, to go to Justice Gorsuch's question of both of my friends earlier, of course, a federal court -- and, here, a court is not sitting -- this is not a post-conviction, you know, AEDPA-type review. The -- there -- the trial court never held this hearing and heard this evidence. It is certainly true that a federal court cannot reverse a state sentence unless it violates the Constitution. That is exactly what both lower courts held. And that has got to be the case because, as Atkins explains, federal constitutional law in this area incorporates state law.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Do you think the Eighth Amendment means something different in Alabama, could mean something different in Alabama than it means in, let's say, Florida or Texas?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: No. I mean, I think --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Okay. So then --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: -- I -- I -- I will -- may I just add one sentence?

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Yes, sure.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: As we've pointed out in our brief in the appendix, and I'm happy to answer questions about any particulars, there is no state -- every state that has considered multiple test scores post-Hall, and it may also be true pre-Hall, I don't know, with the possible -- with the limited exception of Oklahoma, and Oklahoma only when there's one score above 76, has said, if there -- if there are multiple scores whose SEM ranges individually reach 70 or below, you consider other evidence that is relevant to intellectual functioning.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: If -- if the --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: That is a consensus.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: -- if the Eighth Amendment has to mean the same thing in every state, then there is the conundrum of Hall's -- I'm sorry -- of Atkins' reference to the discretion that is afforded to the states. And one possible interpretation of that seems to be the one that you are advocating, which is that federal law somehow incorporates state law so that in -- in determining whether there's an Eighth Amendment violation, the court is to determine whether there was a violation of state law. But isn't it another -- isn't another possible interpretation of that the following? That the Eighth Amendment, as understood in Atkins, gives the states discretion, but there is an Eighth Amendment floor, and the Eighth Amendment floor is what cannot be -- you can't go below the floor. And the floor is whatever a -- a -- a definition of "intellectual disability," procedures for determining intellectual disability that are impermissible. And so long as a state does not go below that floor, a state court does not go below that floor, there's no Eighth Amendment violation.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I'm not sure -- it seems to me so long as the state court does not apply their -- the "means to enforce this constitutional rule," which is the language of Atkins, in a way that is inconsistent with the -- the Atkins principle that persons whom a court, interpreting court law that itself is applying the Eighth Amendment standard, considers to be satisfying of all three prongs. In other words, what the -- what --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: So I -- I take -- I take that as an agreement that -- that it would be possible for a state to come up with procedures that would violate the Eighth Amendment, right? I mean, you wouldn't want to rule that possibility out?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Oh, of course. I mean, that was exactly what happened in Hall and in Moore for that matter and in Brumfield. And in every single one of those cases, the Court remanded to the state courts, which then heard all of the relevant evidence, not just IQ test scores, all of the relevant evidence, and concluded that the defendant had, in fact, satisfied his burden to show all three prongs.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Mr. Waxman, if a state law goes above what we might say the Eighth Amendment floor is and a federal habeas court deviates from what state law requires, does it violate the Eighth Amendment?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: No. I mean, the -- the example would be let's say a state said: Look, we've read all the other cases, but in our state, we're not going to execute anybody who has a true IQ below 80 and we're going to hear all the evidence, but a true IQ below 80, you win under state law. A federal court couldn't say --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Couldn't grant habeas in that circumstance.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Right. Unless there was a compelling argument that the true IQ was below 70. Yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Right. Right. But it might be a violation of state law, but it isn't a violation of the federal Constitution.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: That's right. But what we have here is a situation in which every state, with the limited, very limited exception of Oklahoma, says you can -- IQ test scores are not the same as -- are not equivalent to substantially sub-average intellectual functioning because that is a condition, not a test score. They are highly probative, considering the standard error of measurement, of what actual IQ is, but --

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: So we don't have to really consider Alabama law because what you're articulating -- what you're articulating is the Eighth Amendment standard as you see it based on what you're pointing to in the consensus of the states. But just to kind of clarify the position on this thing we've been going back and forth with about what is the role of state law, really, Alabama law here does not matter to your argument?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Alabama law matters a great deal to my argument because, as this Court has -- has taught, we leave it to the states to -- to apply appropriate means for enforcing the Eighth Amendment guarantee. And that is what the Alabama appellate courts have said repeatedly that they are doing in considering both evidence of IQ test scores if there are any and where those scores are determined by -- upon expert testimony to be inconclusive, other evidence of intellectual functioning, including in this case four other psychometric tests that test intellectual functioning and a history where, in the seventh grade, he was diagnosed, medically diagnosed, as mentally retarded. And considering all those factors, the court found -- both the district court and the court of appeals made clear on remand that with respect to evidence of intellectual functioning, including but not limited to test scores, he satisfied prong 1. The court of appeals then went on to say in its opinion on remand for this Court that "the additional evidence" -- I'm quoting from page 8A of the Joint Appendix, which is quoting from Hall -- the addition -- "additional evidence of Smith's adaptive deficiencies plausibly supports the district court's reading that although Smith scored above" 80 -- "70 in IQ tests, his actual functioning is comparable to that of individuals with a lower score."

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Waxman.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Putting -- putting aside how it applies in this case, the SG's methodological approach, as I understood it, I want to make sure whether you agree or disagree with it, what I understood the Solicitor General to say is that the scores -- start with the scores, but then you have to go on usually, maybe always, to consider all the other evidence, but then, when you make the final evaluation, you can't forget about the scores as part of the overall analysis.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: That is -- I agree 100 percent with that. And the district court did nothing of the sort.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Right. On that latter part, I know you disagree.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Yeah, yeah.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: But, on the methodology, you -- anything you disagree with the Solicitor General on?

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: As I just --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: My memory is even worse than the --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Okay. I'll push it -- I'll push it a little further. I mean, it's possible that the place you do disagree is I think that the Solicitor General had an exception for scores that were -- this is his -- in the brief, the, you know, many 90 scores. And he said, you know, the scores can be such that you wouldn't have to. I think he had that exception --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- that caveat.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I agree with that. I mean, you can take an example, for example, of somebody -- I'm not sure if I've exactly got his hypo right, but let's say four 90s and one 71.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: The court would certainly hear expert testimony on that. There may very well be expert testimony, maybe even agreement, that the 71 is, in fact, the result of malingering or that it is a statistical outlier and taking into account other evidence relevant to intellectual functioning should be disregarded. But I think a constitutional -- a state court that said: Come on, the guy has four 90s and one 71, and I've heard expert testimony on both sides, and I find on the basis of that that the defendant has not satisfied his burden of proving by a preponderance. What we have here are four of five scores --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: On -- on that hypothetical, could --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: If I may just finish my sentence?

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, yes, sure.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: What we have here are two experts who said four of the five scores are within the range of intellectual disability. The -- the district court itself specifically found that. And so we're not in that hypothetical range, but I agree with the Solicitor General.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: On that hypothetical, three or four sentences later -- on that hypothetical, would it be --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I couldn't contain myself. I'm sorry. I apologize.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: I know. Never. Would it be permissible for a district court to say: Well, you got four 90s and one 71, that's enough?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: That's enough, what?

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: That's enough to show -- to -- to show substantial, significantly substantial, substandard -- significantly substandard intellectual functioning. It could do that in that situation?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I mean, it -- the -- the review is for clear error, and unless there were some compelling testimony to discount the four 90s, it would be clear error.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: There's no situation in which IQ scores could be -- in and of themselves be sufficient. So the court could say, look, I've looked at these IQ scores and that's enough. The statistical possibility that the true IQ is below 70 is so small that the -- the defendant can't possibly prevail?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I mean, I'm not aware --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: The court always has to hear additional evidence about adaptive functioning?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I -- I don't think that's the case. And, in fact --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: I thought you said just the opposite, is that not right? You said the court in that case would not have to look at adaptive functioning evidence.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Right. I mean, if -- there's going to be expert testimony pro and con with respect to the administration of these tests. And if the court hears the expert testimony and says that, on balance, I credit the expert who says that the 71 was a result of malingering, or that the 71 is an outlier, or that the 71 tested, as my friend, the Solicitor General's assistant, says, tested certain aspects of intellectual functioning but not others that are really important and it should be discounted, a district court that credited that testimony would not err under the Eighth Amendment --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: What you say --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: -- so long as it was consistent with state law.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: What you say in your brief is that there is near unanimous consensus of the states that multiple IQ scores must be assessed holistically in light of all relevant evidence.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: That's correct. And --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: So, if there are multiple IQ scores, there are five 100s and one 71, you still -- the court can't just say the defendant has not -- cannot prove that he's intellectually disabled. You would -- the court would have to hear additional evidence?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: The court would certainly have to hear expert testimony about the reliability of the -- what are we up to now, five -- five hundreds? But a court would certainly be within -- assuming state law permitted it, a court would certainly be within its -- its rights to say there is no other evidence of intellectual functioning that could reasonably convince me that this man, who fall -- doesn't fall remotely within the borderline even taking the most generous standard error of measurement, and I'm ending my inquiry there. You would have to --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And so what you're --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: You would have to be able to show evidence on one side or the other that the test scores are, in fact, properly -- were properly administered and reliably interpreted. And there are Alabama cases in which the state has offered extraneous evidence, that is, evidence other than test scores, that go to intellectual functioning to say, even though he scored within the range of 69, he's not intellectually disabled.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And so I think your -- your arguments or your responses to Justice Alito are all really heavily focused on the fact that what the court is actually doing is hearing evidence, that the court is actually assessing making this determination about intellectual functioning in light of expert testimony about these things. And so it seems kind of odd to have rules that would be rigid with respect to how these particular experts are testifying or what the impact of the various scores are in this environment, I think. I mean, am I right about -- like, part of the confusion that I'm having is that the -- you know, Alabama and the SG seem to want to remove the evidentiary aspect of this and make it into just a question of -- of law as though the court, without evidence, is just making this decision, when, in fact, the court is hearing from experts who have looked at the test scores and all of the evidence and are testifying as to their beliefs regarding this particular legal criteria.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Let --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And then the court is deciding.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Let me make two points in response to that, Justice Jackson. First of all, it's too late and irrelevant for me to pick sides in Moore versus Texas, but what the district court did in this case is precisely what the Chief Justice in dissent said was properly done in Texas. A court hears the evidence, all probative, relevant, and admissible evidence on both sides, and makes a determination against the standard, the Eighth Amendment standard, with whatever procedural or substantive applications that state law provides. And in this -- in this particular case, what I understand the state to be saying is, look, as a matter of Alabama law, you know, whether you're talking about, I don't know, the -- the overlap or the average or the median or whatever, you know, if you don't have an actual score at 70, you don't get to -- it is error, clear error, for the court to hear other evidence that is probative of intellectual functioning, including other psychometric tests that test intelligence, as well as adaptive functioning, and the fact that the guy was diagnosed as mentally retarded in the seventh grade. Of course, a court should be able to determine that. There is -- there's just no warrant for this Court to announce a constitutional rule binding on all states that precludes consideration of evidence that Alabama and the other states and the medical community properly considers probative of intellectual functioning.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I don't -- well, I think that would be setting a floor for all states, not binding the states. The states could always go above the floor. But, in any event, I understood Alabama and the Solicitor General to have different views on that and that Alabama has that view that you just said, that if the scores -- the median or the overlap is above 70, then that's it, you can't make out a successful Eighth Amendment claim. I understood the Solicitor General to say, no, you still go on and consider the other evidence, but don't forget about the scores. I gather, methodologically, you agree with the Solicitor General. You think Alabama is wrong on that.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, I think --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: That's not really a question, but you can say yes or no to that.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Yes, because, number one, there was -- there is no Alabama case that -- and -- and there was no evidence in this case with respect to whatsoever applying some aggregate score as the test for true IQ, absolutely not. Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: But then -- but then --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: -- median, you know, there --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Counsel, let Justice Kagan --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Oh, I'm sorry, Justice --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: You -- you can have one sentence.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I will -- I will reserve my one sentence to answer your question. And I apologize.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Graver doesn't get a chance to stand up and tell me whether I'm wrong about my summary of his, but then I think he would say, okay, you agree on that, but -- but he says that the court here made two fundamental errors. One is it treated the preponderance standard as a possibility standard, and the second is it never -- it thought that it was not permissible for it to get back around to the scores. And he says that those were the two errors. Were they? Is that right?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: No. I think --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Graver --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: -- I think that the district court's opinion refutes both of those contentions. The district court plainly and expressly found by a preponderance of the evidence that he satisfied prongs 1, 2, and 3 independently. I don't even remember what the second part is, but that would be --

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: No, so the second part is important because Mr. Graver says that the court thought that it couldn't get back around --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Oh.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: -- and say, look, there are five scores here, most of them don't reach the 70 threshold, that sort of thing.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I mean, the district court opinion will speak for itself. The court -- you know, the court said that he showed -- I mean, the court's opinion discusses each test score. It discusses each of the four experts' testimony. It discusses what the experts showed about the other psychometric intelligence tests, about his history, and what each expert said about that, and then concluded, applying Alabama's standard of substantially significant -- substantially sub-average intellectual functioning, that he -- by a preponderance, he had met that standard.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: When you talk about that standard as Alabama's, that's straight out of Atkins, isn't it?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: It's straight out of the Alabama statute.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Also straight out of Atkins, isn't it, and Hall --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: -- and Moore?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: -- At -- Atkins --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: And you say it's the same standard. Everybody except for Oklahoma uses it. So --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, Oklahoma uses --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: -- presumably, they all -- all use it for a reason because it came out of our decisions, right?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, no. The reason it came out of Atkins was Atkins found that there was unanimity among the expert practitioners and associations.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Fine. Whether the -- the -- the chicken or the egg came first, it's in Atkins.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: We're -- we're in violent agreement.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: All right. Thank you.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, Mr. Waxman, I don't do this often, but Justice Gorsuch set out a form -- a -- a statement that he asked the other side whether it was a holding that they disagreed with. You read something.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: I did.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Yes, you did.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Do you want me to read it again?

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Please.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Oh, sure.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Because I'd like Mr. Waxman --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I don't remember it, so I appreciate it.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, and I took it down and I can't find my notes now. So could you please, if -- if you'd do that --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: I would -- I would be delighted.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: The question is just how to understand, which I've struggled with, both Moore and Hall. And I read Hall as saying that you cannot treat a single score in the low 70s as decisive. And I read Moore as saying you can't use facts extraneous to IQ to outweigh a low score. Fair?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Fair with the caveat that extraneous to IQ -- well, no.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: I mean, that's what the Court did --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: If fair -- if what you mean is extraneous to the question of what his true intellectual functioning is --

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: No, no, no.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: -- yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah, it started looking at things to try to outweigh a low score. It started grabbing at other facts.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, it is not only -- it -- it -- I think it would be a due process violation for a court to say the evidence on the test scores is inconclusive, but I'm not going to let you -- I'm not going to let either the state or the defense put on evidence. I mean, this -- this Court has a -- I mean, Washington versus Texas and Crane versus Kentucky and any number of other cases has -- basically stands for the proposition that you can't artificially limit presentation of evidence that is relevant, probative, and admissible.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: In Moore, counsel, the two scores were 74 and 78, and the Court -- and we held the state court violated Hall when it discounted the lower end of the SEM because he was "malingering." There, we held that when there's an IQ below 70 with the SEM, that -- adjusted for the test's standard error, then you must permit evidence of whether clinically he falls within the range of intellectual functioning details, correct?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: That is a correct holding. And my understanding of the disagreement between the majority and the -- and the dissent was, leaving aside the Briseno factors, whether a district judge committed clear error in determining the case the way -- determining the issue the way it did.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And so Justice Gorsuch's second reading is wrong. We do require clinical evidence, but the finder of fact could rightly not be persuaded by it or it could be persuaded by it. That's your point, isn't it?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: That's correct, unless the Court concludes that I've got -- you know, let's make it now 10 IQ test scores, all of which both experts agree were properly administered and reliable. And in light of that finding, one test score of 70 -- there's no -- there is no evidence other than the testimony of the experts that I would -- that could change my mind that he has not met prong 1 --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But that's not --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: -- and I think that --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- the case here.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: We're not in the same time zone as that case.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, because we have three tests, all of them --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Four.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Four, all of them within the SEM. He could be 70 or below?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: He could, and he, in fact, demonstrated by a preponderance that he is.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And, in fact, Dr. King, whom they rely upon, the Petitioner, testified that the scores were within intellectual disability, but then he went to other factors to say why he was -- he thought he was functioning within.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Well, I don't -- I don't -- I think Dr. King's testimony will stand for itself. The district court found his testimony to be less credible than the other experts. What he said is we have five scores and they're all above 70. Four of them, looking at the individual standard error of measurement, are within the range. But he was asked a question by the prosecutor: Are you aware of a theory by Dr. Flynn that when there are multiple test scores that are close together, the standard error of measurement will be reduced? And he said: Yes, I'm aware of that. There was no testimony about what in this case that standard error of measurement would be, and it is inconsistent, flatly inconsistent with what this Court said in Hall and what at least five cases Alabama courts have done, which is always apply plus or minus five. CHIEF JUDGE ROBERTS: Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And, in fact, they -- they've rejected Flynn, correct?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I'm sorry?

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The district court reflect -- rejected the Flynn effect in this case?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Yes. We're not relying on the Flynn effect.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas? Justice Alito?

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: It would help me to get clarification of your position on a couple of issues. I thought you -- I took from your brief and from your answers at an earlier point in the argument that whenever there are multiple IQ scores they must be assessed holistically in light of all relevant evidence. And by relevant evidence, relevant evidence would include evidence about adaptive functions. That's how I understood your brief. That's what I understood you to say at an earlier point in the argument. But then you just recently said, if there is a case where there are, let's say, five 100s and one 71, the inquiry could be cut off at the point -- it would not have to get into any adaptive function. The inquiry could be cut off at that point. Is that correct?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I think, considering the expert testimony, if a court --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Expert testimony about the IQs or about other things?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: About the IQ test administration --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: -- and meaning of the test, a court could say, look, nothing -- nothing you can offer is going to convince me that, in fact, his true IQ is 70 or below. That would be measured -- that determination would be measured for clear error. In other words, what the court would be saying is yes, of course, each side is entitled to offer --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Okay. So there could not be a categorical rule, there can never be a categorical rule that IQs plus testimony about the IQs is sufficient, and a court would err if it went beyond that and opened up the possibility of holding that there was not intellectual disability? You said it would -- the court could do it.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: What I --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: And it would be reviewed for clear error.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: What I am saying is, if there are multiple -- one or more IQ test scores that are within the range of intellectual disability, that is, 70 or below, it is -- it is error for a court to refuse to consider expert testimony and evidence relating to other psychometric tests and school and prior diagnoses to determine whether the defendant meets prong -- his burden to show prong 1.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: So, in just about every case then, IQs and testimony about IQs can never be sufficient?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I -- I don't know how to -- I've given you every possible answer that I have. I think a court looking at nine -- you know, whatever it is, five hundreds and one 71, considering the testimony about how those tests were administered and what the reliable results of those tests are, could conclude, look, I just -- that's it for me. There's nothing you could show me that would change my mind.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: I'm still perplexed also about your view regarding the relevance of Alabama law here. Does it matter what Alabama law says on these points, or are we looking for the minimum Eighth Amendment standard?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: What Alabama law says about how this is or isn't proven is the Eighth Amendment commandment of this Court in Atkins. That is, it allows states to have -- to apply various standards in order to make a determination about the Eighth Amendment violation or not. As we know, the state can apply different levels of burdens consistent with the Due Process Clause. There are some states that say, you know, if somebody comes -- if somebody is charged with a crime and comes in with a score of 65 even before the trial, he is presumptively ineligible. I mean, there are lots of ways in which states have established rules to enforce "the constitutional mandate we announce."

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: One final question. How are we to determine what the national consensus is based on state court decisions that have struggled, that are struggling to understand what this Court has said in Hall and Moore?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: The -- the state has cited one and only one case in which the court has expressed perplexity, and that is not even a state court. It is a federal district court applying the federal death penalty in the Eastern District of -- of -- of New York. The -- you can look at every single --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: All right. Let -- let me just ask it another way, and then I'll give up.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: How are we to determine --

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Or I'll give up.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: The states -- the states are trying -- the state courts are trying to apply our decisions. And would you deny the fact that that's what they're doing? They're just going off and deciding what they think is the -- the morally appropriate way of dealing with this question, or are they trying to understand what this Court has said? And if they're trying to understand what this Court has said, how can that show a national consensus in the sense in which we've used that term in prior death penalty cases?

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: The state courts have uniformly looked to Hall versus Florida and this Court's announcement of its rule in Hall, its two-part holding in Hall versus Florida, and said to determine substantially sub-average intellectual functioning, we look at all probative evidence, including, of course, the test results from properly administered IQ tests.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: All right. Thank you. Thank you.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor? Justice Kagan? Justice Gorsuch? Justice Kavanaugh? Justice Barrett? Justice Jackson?

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Can I just quickly point out that in response to -- to Justice Alito, he asked about whether or not there could ever be a categorical rule that scores alone are enough -- are or are not. And I guess I'm just worried about the clinicians and the people who try to evaluate this condition. And they seem to be saying -- I'm talking about the amicus briefs here of the American Association on Intellectual Disabilities -- that there are "too many variables to consider in determining the validity of each test score and the convergence of that score with other scores and qualitative information to rationally support the use of only one procedure." They seem to be saying that you've got to look at a lot of different things in order to come to the truth of whether or not this person is actually intellectually disabled. It's not enough just as a clinical matter, as a sort of -- outside of the context of the case -- if we're really trying to get to the bottom of this, you have to look at a lot of things. And I thought that was the thrust of the court's conclusion, that you don't cut people off just because they have a particular test score, that you allow experts to evaluate everything and the court to weigh everything in making this assessment.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: That -- that is, in fact, what all clinicians believe, but, of course, there are clinicians who would testify -- you know, who would and, in fact, have testified in Alabama cases that -- there -- there was one case, I'm forgetting the name of it, where the test score was 69. It was only one test. It was 69. And the defense's expert witness testified that she did not believe that, taking into account all of the evidence, he was, in fact, intellect --

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Sure. That's the point of an expert.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: They -- they look at the test scores, they look at all the information, and they make this determination. So sometimes it's not helpful to have categorical rules that impinge on that assessment.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: I -- I think when in -- I mean, I think we have to be mindful, as this Court, members of this Court have frequently reminded us, that clinicians determine clinical evidence -- evaluate clinical evidence and courts apply the law. In this case, we have a law announced in Atkins that someone with this condition may not constitutionally be subject to capital punishment. When in -- there -- there is no court that I'm aware of that has simply said there is no circum- -- you know, there are -- there -- there is a cate- -- well, other than Oklahoma, there is a categorical rule. When and if, you know, a court in Alabama or elsewhere is presented with an argument that there should be a categorical rule or that state law incorporates and the Eighth Amendment permits a categorical rule, that will be for the lower federal courts and perhaps this Court to evaluate.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: MR. WAXMAN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Rebuttal, Mr. Overing?

turns.name: MR. OVERING
turns.text: I had a lot of questions about Alabama law. There's no statute in Alabama. It's Ex Parte Perkins. And it sets the line at 70. The Eleventh Circuit understood that at page 35 when it said Smith's claim turns on whether his IQ is 70 or below. Smith's counsel has essentially abandoned that misreading of Alabama law from their briefs because he agreed with Justice Gorsuch that the question is, is there an Eighth Amendment violation? The United States, at the bottom of page 19 of their brief, said that states can require a defendant to prove his IQ scores betray deficient intellectual functioning. We agree with that. The Eighth Amendment doesn't preclude an IQ burden, and 20 states as amicus agree with us here. Smith's counsel in the Eleventh Circuit said that four and five scores are in the range. Both experts -- this is JA 83 and JA 221 -- use the Flynn effect and then subtracted points for the error range. Both of those are not Alabama law, and they didn't consider the cumulative effect, so the Court should reverse. The Court should remember that this is not just about state sovereignty but also preserving the essential function of the jury to make a reasoned, moral judgment about the criminal and his crime. The jury here had the very best evidence of his intelligence, his 72, the best evidence in his favor. And what's happened decades since then has only confirmed what the jury knew then, that he is not intellectually disabled. The federal courts now have made the demeaning judgment that those 12 everyday Americans rendered a sentence that was so barbarous as to be beyond the limits of civilized society? No. The Court should reverse and give the lower courts no more chances, instruct that the writ of habeas corpus be denied.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
